title: Listening - Part 4
description: For each question, choose the correct answer.
instructions: You will hear an interview. For questions 20–25, choose the answer (A, B or C) which fits best according to what you hear.
context: You will hear an interview with a woman called Maya Patel, who designs apps for teenagers.
audio_script: Interviewer: Our guest today is Maya Patel, who designs apps for teenagers. Maya, how did you first get interested in technology? || Guest: My parents thought I was just playing games on the family tablet, but I was curious about how they worked. At school there was a coding club, and I nearly didn't go because most of my friends chose drama. [20]The teacher let us change a simple game, and seeing my own idea appear on the screen was what excited me[/20]. || Interviewer: Your first successful app helped students organise homework. Why that idea? || Guest: I wasn't the most organised student myself. I tried paper diaries and phone alarms, but I ignored both. [21]My younger sister kept forgetting project dates, so I built something simple for her before making it public[/21]. || Interviewer: What was the biggest problem when many people started using it? || Guest: I thought the technical side would fail, but it didn't. The problem was comments from users. Some were useful, but others were quite rude. [22]Learning not to take every complaint personally was the hardest lesson[/22]. || Interviewer: How do you decide what to include in a new app? || Guest: I don't sit alone guessing. I visit schools and talk to students. Adults often ask for serious study tools, which are useful, but [23]teenagers usually want something quick and easy that doesn't feel like extra homework[/23]. That's important to remember. || Interviewer: Do you enjoy working with a team? || Guest: Yes, though at first I wanted to control everything. Now I have two designers and a sound engineer helping me. [24]The sound engineer made our reminder noises much less annoying, which improved the app more than I expected[/24]. || Interviewer: What would you like to do next? || Guest: People keep asking for a fitness app, but there are already hundreds. [25]I'd rather make an app that helps new students feel confident when they move to a different school[/25]. I remember finding that difficult myself. || Interviewer: Before we finish, Maya, do you think every teenager should learn to code? || Guest: Not necessarily to become a programmer. I think learning a little coding is useful because it teaches you how to break a problem into smaller steps. That helps with lots of subjects, not just technology. But I don't like it when students feel they must be brilliant at computers. Some of my best ideas have come from people who say they are not technical at all. They simply explain what annoys them, and then we build a solution. || Interviewer: Where do you usually work? || Guest: Mostly at home or in a small shared office. Working at home is comfortable, but I need people around me sometimes or I start checking the same tiny details for too long. In the shared office there are people making videos, websites and music, so conversations there give me new ideas. || Interviewer: What do your parents think now? || Guest: They still don't understand everything I do, but they're proud. My dad tests every version of my apps, although he is definitely not the target user. He often finds mistakes because he presses buttons in ways no teenager ever would, so he's surprisingly helpful. || Interviewer: Finally, what do you do when you need new ideas? || Guest: I try to step away from the main work for a short time. Going for a walk, visiting another place or talking to someone outside the job usually helps. If I sit at a desk worrying, nothing improves. I also keep a small notebook, because useful ideas often arrive at inconvenient moments, like on a bus or while making dinner.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Our guest today is Maya Patel, who designs apps for teenagers. Maya, how did you first get interested in technology?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: My parents thought I was just playing games on the family tablet, but I was curious about how they worked. At school there was a coding club, and I nearly didn't go because most of my friends chose drama. The teacher let us change a simple game, and seeing my own idea appear on the screen was what excited me.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Your first successful app helped students organise homework. Why that idea?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I wasn't the most organised student myself. I tried paper diaries and phone alarms, but I ignored both. My younger sister kept forgetting project dates, so I built something simple for her before making it public.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What was the biggest problem when many people started using it?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I thought the technical side would fail, but it didn't. The problem was comments from users. Some were useful, but others were quite rude. Learning not to take every complaint personally was the hardest lesson.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: How do you decide what to include in a new app?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I don't sit alone guessing. I visit schools and talk to students. Adults often ask for serious study tools, which are useful, but teenagers usually want something quick and easy that doesn't feel like extra homework. That's important to remember.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Do you enjoy working with a team?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Yes, though at first I wanted to control everything. Now I have two designers and a sound engineer helping me. The sound engineer made our reminder noises much less annoying, which improved the app more than I expected.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What would you like to do next?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: People keep asking for a fitness app, but there are already hundreds. I'd rather make an app that helps new students feel confident when they move to a different school. I remember finding that difficult myself.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Before we finish, Maya, do you think every teenager should learn to code?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Not necessarily to become a programmer. I think learning a little coding is useful because it teaches you how to break a problem into smaller steps. That helps with lots of subjects, not just technology. But I don't like it when students feel they must be brilliant at computers. Some of my best ideas have come from people who say they are not technical at all. They simply explain what annoys them, and then we build a solution.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Where do you usually work?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Mostly at home or in a small shared office. Working at home is comfortable, but I need people around me sometimes or I start checking the same tiny details for too long. In the shared office there are people making videos, websites and music, so conversations there give me new ideas.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What do your parents think now?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: They still don't understand everything I do, but they're proud. My dad tests every version of my apps, although he is definitely not the target user. He often finds mistakes because he presses buttons in ways no teenager ever would, so he's surprisingly helpful.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Finally, what do you do when you need new ideas?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I try to step away from the main work for a short time. Going for a walk, visiting another place or talking to someone outside the job usually helps. If I sit at a desk worrying, nothing improves. I also keep a small notebook, because useful ideas often arrive at inconvenient moments, like on a bus or while making dinner.

questions.question: What made Maya excited about coding at first?

options.B: using her family tablet at home

questions.explanation: Maya says seeing her own idea appear on the screen after changing a game excited her.

questions.question: Why did Maya create her first successful app?
questions.explanation: She built the app for her younger sister, who kept forgetting project dates.

questions.question: What did Maya find most difficult when the app became popular?
questions.explanation: Maya says learning not to take every complaint personally was the hardest lesson.

questions.question: What does Maya say teenagers usually prefer in an app?
questions.explanation: She says teenagers want something quick and easy that does not feel like extra homework.

questions.question: How did the sound engineer help Maya's app?

options.B: by making the app work faster

questions.explanation: Maya says the sound engineer made the reminder noises less annoying.

questions.question: What kind of app does Maya want to make next?

options.A: one that helps students in a new school
options.B: one that encourages people to exercise
options.C: one that teaches people how to code

questions.explanation: Maya says she would rather make an app that helps new students feel confident when they move school.
